content.text: What Is the AWS Console Mobile App?

The AWS Console Mobile App is a free iOS and Android application that gives you read-heavy access to your AWS account from a phone or tablet. It is not a full replacement for the browser-based Management Console — it covers a targeted subset of services focused on monitoring, status checking, and a small set of operational actions like starting and stopping EC2 instances.
The app is designed for two primary use cases:
on-call incident response
(checking service health and alarm state when away from a desk) and
passive monitoring

(glancing at billing spend or EC2 instance counts during the day without opening a laptop). It is particularly useful for engineers in on-call rotations who need to assess the severity of an incident from their phone before deciding whether to log into the full console.
Feature Overview

attrs.alt: AWS Console Mobile App showing dashboard with health events, cost, alarms, and EC2 counts alongside a CloudWatch alarm view with push notifications

content.text: The app surfaces five primary areas of information on its home dashboard: Health Dashboard events, CloudWatch alarm state, billing cost-to-date, EC2 instance counts, and recent support case updates. Each widget is tappable and drills into a service-specific detail view.
Supported Services and Capabilities
The mobile app covers a focused set of services. Understanding what is and is not supported prevents frustration when you need to perform a task on mobile.
What You Can Do
Service
Supported Actions
AWS Health
View active events and scheduled changes affecting your account
CloudWatch
View alarm states (OK / ALARM / INSUFFICIENT_DATA), acknowledge alarms, view metric graphs
Billing & Cost Management
View month-to-date spend, budget alerts, cost by service
EC2
List instances, view instance details, start / stop / reboot individual instances
CloudFormation
View stack status and events (CREATE_COMPLETE, ROLLBACK_IN_PROGRESS, etc.)
Elastic Beanstalk
View environment health and recent events
RDS
View instance status, recent events, and basic metrics
OpsWorks
View stack and layer status
Support
View open support cases, add correspondence
What Is Not Supported
The app is intentionally scoped to monitoring and lightweight actions. The following are not available in the mobile app and require the browser-based console, CLI, or SDK:
Creating, modifying, or deleting most resource types (VPCs, security groups, S3 buckets, Lambda functions, RDS instances, IAM users/roles/policies)
CloudShell or any terminal/CLI access
S3 object management (upload, download, browse objects)
Lambda function deployment or code viewing
IAM administration
Full CloudWatch Logs browsing or Insights queries
Resource tagging or Tag Editor
CloudFormation stack creation or updates
Design principle:

The mobile app prioritizes safety. Allowing unrestricted resource creation and deletion from a mobile device (where accidental taps are common) introduces too much risk. The read-focused design means the worst a mobile user can do by accident is restart an EC2 instance.
Setting Up the Mobile App
Installation
The app is available as:
AWS Console — iOS: search "AWS Console" in the App Store (publisher: Amazon Web Services)
AWS Console — Android: search "AWS Console" in the Google Play Store (publisher: Amazon Web Services)
Download is free. The app does not require a separate account — you sign in with the same IAM user or root account credentials you use in the browser.
Authentication
The app supports three authentication methods:
IAM user credentials (username and password). After entering your account ID and IAM username, you enter your password. If MFA is enabled on the account, the app prompts for an MFA code.

Federated identity via IAM Identity Center (AWS SSO). If your organization uses SSO, enter your SSO start URL (e.g., https://mycompany.awsapps.com/start) and authenticate through your identity provider's mobile browser flow.
Root account credentials. Not recommended — use an IAM user or role instead.

After authentication, the app stores a session token. Sessions expire per your IAM or SSO session duration policy (typically 1–12 hours). The app prompts you to re-authenticate when the session expires.
Multi-Account Setup
If you manage multiple AWS accounts, the mobile app lets you add and switch between accounts:
App menu → Accounts → Add Account
Enter the account ID (12-digit number), then sign in with the appropriate credentials
Switch accounts via the account selector at the top of the home screen
Each account is saved separately with its own authentication state
CloudWatch Alarms on Mobile

CloudWatch alarm monitoring is the most-used feature of the mobile app among on-call engineers. The app shows all alarms in the selected account and region with their current state, and you can configure push notifications so your phone alerts you when an alarm transitions to ALARM state.
Viewing Alarms
The CloudWatch section groups alarms by state: ALARM (red), INSUFFICIENT_DATA (yellow), OK (green). Tapping an alarm shows:
The alarm description and threshold (e.g., CPUUtilization > 80% for 5 consecutive periods)
The current metric value and whether the condition is currently met
A sparkline graph of the metric over the last hour
The alarm's action configuration (which SNS topic is notified, which Auto Scaling policy triggers)
Recent state transition history
Push Notifications for Alarms
To receive push notifications when alarms fire:
In the mobile app: Settings → Notifications → Enable push notifications.
The app creates an SNS endpoint for your device automatically.
Navigate to the specific CloudWatch alarm you want to monitor.
Tap the alarm → Subscribe to notifications.
The app subscribes your device to the alarm's SNS topic.

Push notifications fire within a few seconds of an alarm state transition. The notification includes the alarm name, the new state (ALARM or OK), and the metric value that triggered the transition. Tapping the notification opens the alarm detail directly.
Limitation:

The app can only subscribe to push notifications for alarms that have an SNS action configured. If an alarm has no SNS action (it only triggers an Auto Scaling policy, for example), you cannot receive push notifications for it from the mobile app.
EC2 Instance Management on Mobile

The EC2 section of the mobile app lists all instances in the selected region with their state (running, stopped, terminated) and basic metadata (instance type, launch time, name tag). From this view you can:
Filter instances by state or name
View instance details: instance ID, type, AMI, public/private IP, security groups, key pair, launch time
Start a stopped instance
Stop a running instance (sends a graceful stop signal, same as the console's "Stop" action)
Reboot a running instance (sends a restart signal without termination)
You cannot terminate instances from the mobile app — termination is a destructive action intentionally excluded from the mobile surface.
Practical Use: Restarting a Hung Process

A common on-call scenario: a monitoring alert fires indicating an application is unresponsive, but SSH is unavailable (security group blocks port 22 from mobile networks). If the application server is an EC2 instance, you can initiate a reboot from the mobile app to trigger a clean restart:
Open the EC2 section in the mobile app.
Find the instance by name filter.
Tap the instance → Reboot.
The instance reboots (30–90 seconds typically for Amazon Linux); wait for monitoring to confirm recovery.

This covers the "I need to do something but can only use my phone" gap without requiring a VPN or laptop. It is not a substitute for proper runbooks but it is a genuine operational safety valve during incidents.
Billing and Cost Monitoring

The Billing section shows your current month-to-date spend and compares it to the previous month's total. Service-level cost breakdown is available: you can see how much each service (EC2, RDS, S3, data transfer, etc.) has cost so far this month.

Budget alerts appear in the app when you have AWS Budgets configured. If you set a budget of $500/month for an account and the month-to-date spend crosses 80% ($400), the Budget alert appears in the mobile app's notification list even without push notification setup — it surfaces in-app when you open the Billing section.
Checking Costs Quickly

The mobile app is faster than the browser-based Cost Explorer for a simple question like "how much have we spent this month?" Opening Cost Explorer in a browser, waiting for the page to load, and navigating to the current month view takes 10–20 seconds. The mobile app's dashboard shows the same number in 2–3 seconds after opening the app.
AWS Health Dashboard on Mobile

The Health section shows active AWS service events that affect your account, scheduled maintenance windows, and recent notifications. This is the first place to check during an incident to determine whether the issue is on AWS's side or your own infrastructure.
The mobile app shows the same data as the AWS Health Dashboard in the browser, including:
Open issues: active service events (e.g., EC2 API degradation in us-east-1)
Scheduled changes: upcoming maintenance that could cause brief interruptions (e.g., RDS minor version upgrade)
Other notifications: informational events like new service launches or deprecation notices

During an incident, checking Health first can save significant debugging time. If Health shows an active EC2 event in your region, you know the root cause is an AWS-side issue — no need to investigate your own code or infrastructure.
Limitations and When to Use the Desktop Console
The mobile app is intentionally narrow in scope. The following situations require the browser-based console:
Situation
Why mobile is insufficient
Deploying new infrastructure
Resource creation wizards are not available on mobile
Debugging Lambda errors
CloudWatch Logs queries and Lambda console not available
Updating security groups
VPC and networking not available on mobile
IAM changes
IAM is not in the mobile app
CloudShell / CLI access
Terminal not available on mobile
Detailed metric analysis
CloudWatch Insights not available; only basic sparklines
S3 file operations
Object management not available on mobile
A good mental model: the mobile app is for
reading state and taking simple stabilizing actions
. Any task that involves creating, updating, or deleting configuration — other than starting, stopping, or rebooting EC2 instances — belongs on the desktop console.
Configuring Push Notifications with SNS

To use the mobile app's push notification feature effectively, you need CloudWatch alarms that are already configured to send notifications to an SNS topic. The mobile app then subscribes your device endpoint to that topic. Here is the full setup path from scratch:
Step 1 — Create an SNS Topic for Alarms

# Create SNS topic for alarm notifications
aws sns create-topic   --name prod-alarm-notifications   --region us-east-1

# Output: TopicArn: arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:prod-alarm-notifications
Step 2 — Create a CloudWatch Alarm with SNS Action

# Create an alarm that notifies the SNS topic when CPU stays above 80% for 5 minutes
aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm   --alarm-name high-cpu-web-server   --alarm-description "CPU above 80% for 5 minutes"   --metric-name CPUUtilization   --namespace AWS/EC2   --statistic Average   --period 300   --evaluation-periods 1   --threshold 80   --comparison-operator GreaterThanThreshold   --dimensions Name=InstanceId,Value=i-0abc1234567890def   --alarm-actions arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:prod-alarm-notifications   --ok-actions arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:prod-alarm-notifications   --region us-east-1
Step 3 — Subscribe the Mobile App
Open the AWS Console Mobile App on your phone:
Settings → Notifications → Enable push notifications (grants iOS/Android notification permission)
The app registers your device with SNS and creates a platform endpoint ARN
Navigate to CloudWatch → Alarms → select the alarm
Tap 'Subscribe to notifications' — the app subscribes your device endpoint to the alarm's SNS topic
From this point, transitions to ALARM state trigger a push notification to your device. The notification payload includes the alarm name, new state, and threshold metric value.
On-Call Workflow Integration
The mobile app fits into a broader on-call workflow. Here is how a typical on-call engineer might use it alongside other tools during an incident:

On-Call Incident Flow (using mobile app):

  [Pager / push notification fires]
  ↓
  1. Read notification: "high-latency-api ALARM — LatencyP99 > 2000ms"
  
  2. Open AWS Console mobile app:
     → Health Dashboard: check for active AWS events in us-east-1
       → No open issues → root cause is likely in our infrastructure
  
  3. CloudWatch Alarms: find high-latency-api
     → Current P99 latency: 3,450ms (was 180ms)
     → Alarm started: 5 minutes ago
     → Linked metrics: also check CPUUtilization on web-server instances
  
  4. EC2 Instances:
     → Filter: "web-server" → 3/4 instances running
     → web-server-02: Stopped (!) — likely cause of latency spike
     → Tap → Start instance
     → Wait 90 seconds for instance to pass status checks
  
  5. Monitor CloudWatch alarm: latency returns to OK within 3 minutes
  
  6. Write incident note in Support case (or Slack from phone)
  
  [Incident resolved — escalation to desktop console not needed]

This flow covers a significant percentage of on-call scenarios that do not require code changes or configuration updates — just identifying and correcting a state problem. The mobile app's limited surface area actually makes it faster for this use case than the full console, which requires navigating multiple pages.
Security Considerations for Mobile Access
Accessing AWS from a mobile device introduces security considerations that do not apply to desktop access:
Screen Lock and App Lock

Enable screen lock (PIN, biometric) on your phone. The mobile app itself does not add a second PIN, so device-level security is your primary protection. On a shared or lost phone, a screen lock is the only thing preventing unauthorized AWS access.
Session Duration

IAM user sessions in the mobile app last as long as the IAM console session policy allows (default is 12 hours for direct IAM user sign-in; shorter for SSO sessions). Consider setting your IAM user's session duration to 4–8 hours so a stolen phone session expires sooner.
MFA Requirement

If your IAM policy requires MFA for sensitive actions (stop/terminate EC2 instances, etc.), the mobile app respects that requirement. You will be prompted for an MFA code when attempting the action. However, configuring MFA-required policies is something you do in IAM, not in the mobile app.
Federated SSO vs. IAM User

Prefer authenticating via IAM Identity Center (SSO) over direct IAM user credentials. SSO sessions are time-limited and tied to your corporate identity provider's session, which means revoking access in your IdP immediately invalidates the mobile app session — useful when an employee leaves or a phone is lost.

Mobile App auth comparison:
  
  IAM user credentials:
    Pro: Works without SSO setup
    Con: Long-lived credentials; revocation requires IAM policy change
    Con: If credentials are compromised, attacker has persistent access
  
  IAM Identity Center (SSO):
    Pro: Session tied to IdP; revoke in IdP → mobile access immediately lost
    Pro: Short-lived tokens (1–12 hour sessions)
    Con: Requires SSO setup in your organization
    Recommended: Yes, for any production account
Comparing Mobile App vs. Browser Console vs. CLI
Knowing which tool to reach for in each situation saves time during incidents:

Situation                           Best tool
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Check if AWS has a service outage    Mobile app (Health Dashboard)
View CloudWatch alarm state          Mobile app
Restart an EC2 instance              Mobile app
Create an EC2 instance               Browser console
Deploy a Lambda function             CLI or browser console
Check billing spend quickly          Mobile app
Analyze cost by service in detail    Browser console (Cost Explorer)
Run shell commands on an instance    CLI via SSH or browser CloudShell
Update a security group              Browser console or CLI
View S3 object contents              CLI (aws s3 cp) or browser console
Check stack deployment status        Mobile app or browser console
IAM role changes                     Browser console or CLI
Troubleshooting the Mobile App
App Shows 'Access Denied' for Some Services
The mobile app uses the same IAM permissions as your sign-in identity. If your IAM user or role lacks
ec2:DescribeInstances

permissions, the EC2 section in the app shows an access denied error. Check the IAM policies attached to your user or role and confirm they include read permissions for the services you need in the app.
Push Notifications Not Arriving
Checklist when push notifications stop working:
Phone: ensure notifications are enabled for the AWS Console app in iOS Settings / Android Settings
App: Settings → Notifications should show 'Enabled'
Alarm: confirm the alarm has an SNS action pointing to the topic you subscribed to

SNS: check that your device endpoint in SNS is still active (endpoints can become inactive if notifications fail repeatedly — iOS APNS and Android FCM return an error that marks the endpoint disabled)
IAM: ensure your IAM identity has sns:Subscribe and sns:SetEndpointAttributes permissions
Session Keeps Expiring Too Quickly

If the app asks you to re-authenticate frequently, check your IAM session duration. For IAM users, the maximum session duration is controlled by the IAM user's policy and the STSGetSessionToken call settings. For SSO sessions, duration is set in IAM Identity Center → Permission Sets → Session duration. Extending it to 8–12 hours reduces re-authentication friction during long shifts.
Summary

The AWS Console Mobile App (available free on iOS and Android) provides a phone-optimized view of AWS account health, CloudWatch alarms with push notifications, billing cost-to-date, EC2 instance status and start/stop/reboot actions, and support case tracking. It is purpose-built for on-call monitoring and lightweight incident response — not resource management. Setting up push notifications for critical CloudWatch alarms turns the mobile app into a pager replacement that gives engineers actionable context (metric value, alarm state, account affected) alongside the alert. For any task that goes beyond status checking or restarting instances, use the full browser-based console or CLI.
